entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in November
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere News in November

Another month with two great new releases ✨

Learn more in my short top features video in November2025 on YouTube 📺 and a recap from SAP TechEd this year in the blog 📝 below, where you also find more details about the foundational learning journey – Becoming an SAP Data Architect.

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.

 

 

My top features in November

 

Define Row-Level Authorizations Based on IdP User Attributes

This small enhancement is very powerful and my favorite feature of November. You can now use values in custom attributes provided by your identity provider as identifiers in your data access controls and define conditions that apply to users with these values. Why is that powerful? Because using these attributes allows you to apply a single condition to all users whose accounts contain the specified attribute value. In other words, you can group users with attributes to assign permissions to a group instead of individual users.

➡️  More information.

 

Cancel the Start or Stop Process of an Elastic Compute Node

When an elastic compute node is starting or stopping, you can now cancel the process. The data is rolled back and restored to the state that existed before the start or stop process was initially triggered.

➡️  More information.

 

Replication Flows: Increase Maximum Parallel Job

You can increase the maximum number of parallel jobs up to 16 to run replication flows. Before, that maximum was 10, so this increase is helpful when you need more parallelization for certain flows.

More information:
➡️  Configure the Size of Your SAP Datasphere Tenant and
➡️  Sizing, Capacity Planning, and Load Balancing for Replication Flows

 

New Incremental Aggregation Types for Transformation Flows in a File Space

The aggregation types MIN, MAX, COUNT*, and AVG have been added to the already existing types LAST, SUM, and COUNT. 

➡️  More information.

 

Transformation Flows: Cancel a Running Merge Task

From the Local Tables (File) monitor, you can now cancel a merge task that is running with a new Cancel button.

➡️  More information.

 

Disable Delta Capture for Local Tables (File)

When creating a local table (file) from the table editor or as a target of your transformation flow, you can disable the delta capture settings.

More information:
➡️  Creating a Local Table (File) and
➡️  Creating a Transformation Flow in a File Space.

 

 

SAP TechEd 2025

I had the opportunity to be onsite in Berlin at SAP TechEd 2025 as well as in Sydney for SAP TechEd on tour. It was great to see so many people in person at both events. After many years of virtual TechEd’s this was excellent. Not only meeting many friends but also having those inspiring discussions with new people from various customers, partners, and also colleagues from SAP.

Session DA203 together with Dominik Kurz was recorded as part of the virtual SAP TechEd and can be watched here: 
🔗  https://www.sap.com/events/teched/virtual/flow/sap/tev25/catalog-virtual/page/catalog/session/1754495722564001nPF0

 

Happy to share that my deep dive session “The impact of SAP Business Data Cloud on SAP Datasphere users” made it to the top 5 most attended sessions overall in Berlin. In general, all sessions around SAP Business Data Cloud drew high participation and our colleagues at the SAP BDC booth were also busy until the last minute on Thursday afternoon.

A highlight was certainly the partnership announcement with Snowflake. The announcement covers two flavors brownfield and greenfield scenarios.
(1) Brownfield: enabling Snowflake systems to be connected via BDC connect, targeted for GA in H1 2026
(2) Greenfield: offering SAP Snowflake on the SAP pricelist as a solution extension, targeted for Q1 2026

A week after TechEd we also announced a partnership with Microsoft at Microsoft Ignite, enabling Microsoft Fabric to be connected via BDC connect, targeted for GA in H3 2026.

That covers now Databricks, Snowflake, Google and Microsoft as technology partners for SAP Business Data Cloud Connect. Not much missing here from my point of view.

 

Becoming an SAP Data Architect - Foundational Learning Journey

 

This is a great learning opportunity for all you data architects out there‼️
Kudos to the learning team together with our architectural experts!

The foundational Learning Journey of becoming an SAP Data Architect is the first step in a comprehensive program designed to empower data professionals and architects of the future. This new learning journey provides a structured path to develop the knowledge, frameworks, and strategic mindset needed to design, govern, and communicate enterprise-scale data architectures.

🧭  What Is the Data Architecture Learning Journey?

The learning journey is built to create strong Data Architecture foundations moving from foundational principles to strategic business impact. Learners will gain both architectural knowledge and understanding that is essential for any modern data architect and covers the following four units:

🔹Foundational Concepts Learn the core principles and frameworks of data architecture from conceptual, logical, and physical data modelling to metadata management and frameworks.

🔹Modern Paradigms & Integration Explore today’s architectural models like Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Lakehouse and design integration strategies (ETL, ELT, and streaming) for scalable, real-time analytics.

🔹Governance, Security & Strategic Impact Build governance frameworks, establish data lineage, safeguard privacy, and align data strategy with business objectives for measurable value.

🔹Communicating Data Value Strengthen communication and storytelling skills to articulate architectural value, design transformation roadmaps, and drive organizational adoption of data-driven change.

❓ Why This Matters

In a world where data is at the core of every business decision, the role of the Data Architect has become critical.

This learning journey helps professionals at every level develop the skills, language, and strategic vision to lead in data architecture and governance.

👉  By completing this journey, learners will be able understand how to:

  1. Design efficient, compliant, and scalable data systems.
  2. Integrate modern architectural paradigms.
  3. Translate architecture into business value.
  4. Build narratives and roadmaps that drive transformation.

➡️  How to Get Started

  1. Visit the learning.sap.com and search for “Data Architect” OR use the Link
    🔗  https://learning.sap.com/courses/becoming-an-sap-data-architect
  2. Enroll to start your journey, Progress through the learning units at your own pace.
  3. Earn a Digital Badge on completion and share it on your socials!

And we have a lots more in store for 2026!

🔗  More information.

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting. Also check out the new support content for SAP Datasphere on SAP Help for troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: SAP BTP: Data Fabric
entry.content:
Irrespective of where your source system resides, SAP provides the capabilities to ingest, harmonize and unlock real-time and predictive insights on your target system with confidence. This is supported by resilient, enterprise-grade frameworks, tools, and methodologies that safeguard data quality, integrity, and consistency throughout the entire data lifecycle.

 SAP. (2023). Figure:1

Source Systems for Data Provisioning in SAP

URL: https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-data-provisioning-in-sap-bw-4hana/identifying-sap-bw-4hana-source-systems-and-their-use-cases

 

The endless possibilities of raw, intrinsic data ingestion from e.g. Enterprise warehouses, data lakes, rational & in-memory databases, SaaS applications even social media into SAP HANA (On-premises and/ or Cloud) opens a world of opportunity for data engineers to acquire rich, accurate, and complete master and transactional data from all possible sources imaginable. Responsibility for managing the data acquisition processes depends on the provisioning type being used. Common approaches include:

Provisioning Type

Data Flow Responsibility

Metadata Management

Typical Use Cases

Application-Controlled

Source application pushes data to target using APIs or connectors.

Managed in application layer (schema, mappings, transformations).

Custom logic, application-driven workflows, SaaS integrations.

Change Data Capture (CDC)

CDC engine streams changes (insert/update/delete) from source to target in near real-time.

Managed by CDC tool (change logs, timestamps, transaction markers).

Real-time replication, incremental updates, event-driven architectures.

Database-Controlled

Target database pulls data from source using adapters or SDI.

Centralized in database layer (schemas, lineage, governance).

Governance-heavy environments, centralized data lakes, enterprise ETL.

Figure:2

Provisioning Types: Data Flow & Metadata Management

 

The utilization of high-performance ETL/ELT tools ensure the consistence, quality and reliability of the data is upheld and thus often used to unify the data to best meet the objectives of the stakeholder’s request either being executives, decision makers, marketing or product. A well-thought-out data provisioning strategy should always take precedence as this ensure a smooth flowing data process. Depending on the usage of the data, a data engineer may explorer the below different data provisioning approaches:

  • Data federation – Data is not loaded nor persisted into the target system (SAP HANA) but rather read directly from the source system only when the data is needed for use.
  • Data replication – Data from the source system is loaded, persisted into the target system and kept in sync using various mechanisms
    • Real-time (Synchronous) replication
    • Delta load (asynchronous)replication
  • Data transformation – The raw data is enhanced with additional features e.g. calculation columns, renamed fields or new fields derived from other exiting fields

SAP provides but not limited to the below high-performance native data integration technologies:

Tool

Primary Function

Position in Landscape Architecture

SLT (SAP Landscape Transformation Replication)

Real-time replication (CDC)

Integration layer between SAP sources and HANA DB

SDA (Smart Data Access)

Virtual access to remote sources (federation)

Integration layer enabling query pushdown to the HANA DB

SDI (Smart Data Integration)

ETL/ELT pipelines, adapters, dataflows

Integration layer moving and transforming data before loading into the HANA DB

Figure 3:

SAP Native ETL/ELT tools

Furthermore, for Data quality, cleansing and enrichment before persisting into the HANA DB, SAP provides the below:

  • Smart Data quality (SDQ) for SAP HANA on-premises
  • Data Quality Management Microservices (DQMm) as a subscription-based microservice for SAP HANA Cloud

 

SAP Hana provides a variation of consolidated tools both on premise and in the cloud, all providing for various functionality for different use cases such as:

  • Supporting data provisioning  
  • Developing Database artefacts
  • Monitoring the performance of the develop artefacts
  • Monitoring the Database and its system performance
  • Administering and managing the Database

One of the key strengths of the SAP ecosystem is the ability to seamlessly combine cloud functionalities together with on-premises and still import specific/ needed applications as micro services into common IDE’s such as Microsoft Visual Studio code is commendable because it:

  • Provides business with a comprehensive 360-degree view of
    • Database health overview.
    • Data life cycle from end to end.
    • Application life cycle from end to end.
  • Empowers the end users, data engineers, developers, data scientists, data analyst and support engineers with greater power and flexibility over the data being used.

Below are native tools used by SAP for various purposes:

  • SAP Business Application studio – Cloud development tool.
  • Web IDE – On-premises development tool
  • SAP Hana cockpit - a web-based tool for monitoring, administering, and managing SAP HANA databases, offering a centralized, browser-accessible interface with tile-based dashboards for performance, resource usage (CPU, memory, disk), alerts, and security. It allows tasks like starting/stopping services, managing users, viewing alerts, analyzing performance, and running SQL queries through its integrated Database Explorer. 

SAP SaaS applications are imported into common IDE, s such as Microsoft Visual Studio code via extensions, libraries etc. for a specific purpose e.g.:

  •  Supportability tools for SAP HANA - For both online and offline performance analysis and troubleshooting. With a focus on the needs of support engineers, the performance analysis tools provide enhanced analysis options for trace files and runtime dump files, as well as allowing in-depth analysis on extensive datasets.
  • The SQL analyzer tool for SAP HANA - A performance analysis tool that helps developers visualize and understand SQL execution plans. Designed for query tuning and optimization, it offers detailed insights into how queries run within the SAP HANA database.
  • SAP HANA Database Explorer - Provides functionality for accessing SAP HANA databases, browsing the database catalog, and executing SQL from a SQL console.

Figure 4:

SAP Development and Administration Tools Overview

 

The consolidated toolset further enhances productivity and convenience for end users, data engineers, developers, data scientists, data analyst and support engineers by enabling them to access all essential capabilities from a single unified platform. The choice and flexibility to work with the desired programming languages ranging from but not limited to: SQL, SQL Script, Python, Node.js, JavaScript and react native etc. Enable a cross-functional team with diverse skill set to collaborate effectively while striving towards the same common goal.

 Figure 5:

Core Programming Languages and Frameworks for SAP HANA Cloud and On-Premises

 

After successfully completing the Explore phase, with a solid fit -to-standard analysis of proposed use cases, Extrinsic data pipelines are then implemented for reliable and consistent data flow. These pipelines support a wide range of business needs such as decision making, go-to-market activities or ML/AI use cases requested by various stakeholders. The process is typically executed and maintained by data engineers, developers, data scientists, data analyst and/or support engineers.

The harmonized data is then governed, analyzed, and visualized through multiple front-end applications. These applications are powered by various integration frameworks e.g. REST APIs, OData v4, JDBC/ODBC, each serving different user needs and functional objectives:

 Figure: 6

SAP-Compatible Applications and Their Integration Frameworks

 

Mutatis mutandis, Data is the inception of all there is, as it has been in the beginning, is now and ever shall be. Thank you all for taking a glimpse into the above Content. Please don’t for get to like, comment and share.

 

Abbreviations:

API: Application Programming Interface

BTP: Business Technology Platform

CDC: Change Data Capture

DB: Database

DQMm: Data Quality Management Microservices

ELT: Extract, Load, Transform

ETL: Extract, Transform, Load

IDE: Integrated Development Environment

ML/AI: Machine Learning/Artificial Intelligence

ODBC: Open Database Connectivity

OData: Open Data Protocol

REST: Representational State Transfer

SaaS: Software as a Service

SDI: Smart Data Integration

SDA: Smart Data Access

SDQ: Smart Data Quality

SLT: SAP Landscape Transformation Replication

SQL: Structured Query Language

 

Reference:

  1. SAP. (2023). Figure:1 Source Systems for Data Provisioning in SAP, URL: https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-data-provisioning-in-sap-bw-4hana/identifying-sap-bw-4hana-source-systems-and-their-use-cases

entry.title: Data masking, data scrambling and data anonymization in Business Data Cloud with SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • 1 - Generalities
  • 1.1 - Data masking
  • 1.2 - Data scrambling
  • 1.2.1 - Create function
  • 1.2.2 - Expose table data
  • 1.2.3 - Create a view
  • 1.2.4 - Import the view into Datasphere
  • 1.3 - Data anonymization
  • 2 - Standardization
  • 2.1 - Parameters table
  • 2.2 - SQL Procedures
  • 2.2.1 - Procedure EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT
  • 2.2.2 - Procedure EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR
  • 2.2.3 - Procedure EXEC_ALL_VIEWS_CREATOR
  • 2.3 - Task Chain
  • 2.3.1 - Grant permissions
  • 2.3.2 - Create a task chain
  • 2.3.3 - Test
  • Conclusion

 

Introduction

This blog post was inspired from: How Data Anonymization in SAP HANA secure Generative AI applications through SAP Datasphere 

Here you will find a description of generalities about:

  • masking, scrambling and anonymization features
  • how to automate the creation of views with data masking, data scrambling and data anonymization and the possibility to activate/deactivate some features in the target views.

SAP Datasphere contains a lot of features. Some are accessible directly via its user interface and others are accessible via Open SQL Schema (see SAP Datasphere help site). Open SQL Schema gives you access to the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database, and extends the capacities offered via Datasphere's UI.

In this blog, we use a Datasphere space called DEMO with the database user DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE.

We will use a Datasphere local table SALARY as an example dataset where we would like to:

  • mask the CREDIT_CARD column and rename the column to CREDIT_CARD_MASKED,
  • scramble the NAME column and rename the column to NAME_SCRAMBLED,
  • anonymize the SALARY column and rename the column to SALARY_ANONYMIZED

 

 

Note: SALARY column data type is Double.

Save the content below to import a sample dataset into the SALARY local table.

"ID","NAME","GENDER","CITY","SALARY","CREDIT_CARD"
"1","Paul McCartney","M","Levallois","1002.32","1111-1111-1111-1111"
"2","Martin Solveig","M","Munich","3218.21","2222-2222-2222-2222"
"3","Nils Lofgren","M","Nice","1398.32","3333-3333-3333-3333"
"4","Annika","F","Munich","2053.23","4444-4444-4444-4444"

 

1 - Generalities

1.1 - Data masking

Data masking is a feature implemented into SAP HANA Cloud (see Data Masking).
In this blog, we will not use privileges to manage masking, but simply use calculated SQL expressions when defining views where some columns should be masked.

To mask the content of the NAME column, we can just use the following SQL Statement into an SQL view object in Datasphere :

SELECT "ID",
   "NAME",
   CITY,
   GENDER,
   SALARY,
   LEFT(CREDIT_CARD, 4) || '-XXXX-XXXX-' || RIGHT(CREDIT_CARD, 4) AS CREDIT_CARD_MASKED
FROM SALARY

The content displayed by the view is:

 

1.2 - Data scrambling

To scramble characters in a string, a function needs to be created in the Open SQL Schema DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE (the one automatically created by Datasphere when creating the database user). To connect to this schema, SAP HANA Database Explorer can be started from the space management window:

 

1.2.1 - Create function

Once in data Database explorer, the function SCRAMBLE_STRING can be created:

Copy/Paste the following code:

CREATE FUNCTION SCRAMBLE_STRING (IN input NVARCHAR(500))
RETURNS scrambled NVARCHAR(500)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE len INT = LENGTH(:input);
    DECLARE temp NVARCHAR(500) = :input;
    DECLARE result NVARCHAR(500) = '';
    DECLARE pos INT;
 
    WHILE LENGTH(:temp) > 0 DO
        pos := CEIL(RAND() * LENGTH(:temp));  -- Pick random position
        result := result || SUBSTRING(:temp, :pos, 1); -- Append character
        temp := REPLACE(:temp, SUBSTRING(:temp, :pos, 1), ''); -- Remove picked char
    END WHILE;
 
    scrambled := result;
  END

1.2.2 - Expose table data

Since Datasphere local tables cannot be directly exposed, in order to be able to read the content of the SALARY table when using an Open SQL Schema, a view V_SALARY needs to be created in Datasphere using the Data Builder:

SELECT "ID",
	"NAME",
	"GENDER",
	"CITY",
	"SALARY",
	"CREDIT_CARD"
FROM "SALARY"

1.2.3 - Create a view

A view V_SALARY_SCRAMBLED can now be created using Database Explorer to scramble the column NAME from the  V_SALARY view (the call to INITCAP function was just added to format names):

CREATE VIEW "V_SALARY_NAME_SCRAMBLED" AS
(SELECT 
	"ID" AS "ID",
	INITCAP(SCRAMBLE_STRING("NAME")) AS NAME_SCRAMBLED,
	GENDER AS GENDER,
	CITY AS CITY,
	SALARY,
	CREDIT_CARD
FROM DEMO.V_SALARY);

1.2.4 - Import the view into Datasphere

You can now import the view to create a Datasphere local table: start the process to create a dummy Datasphere view, select the Sources tab in the source browser, then click on the schema name and Drag and Drop of the View name to the text area. This triggers the table import, click on Import an Deploy, then close the New SQL View object (no need to save it, click on Discard):

Now, the view "DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE".V_SALARY_SCRAMBLED can be opened and used in Datasphere:

 

1.3 - Data anonymization

The SAP HANA Data Anonymization Guide explains how to anonymize data. In this blog, we will not focus on the technique used to anonymize data, but rather on sample syntax used to anonymize data. The following SQL code creates a view with the anonymized SALARY column (the view with anonymization will be effective after the REFRESH command):

CREATE VIEW "V_SALARY_SALARY_ANONYMIZED" AS
(SELECT 
	"ID",
	"NAME",
	GENDER,
	CITY,
	SALARY AS SALARY_ANONYMIZED,
	CREDIT_CARD
FROM DEMO.V_SALARY)
WITH ANONYMIZATION(
	ALGORITHM 'DIFFERENTIAL_PRIVACY'
	PARAMETERS '{"data_change_strategy": "qualified"}'
	COLUMN "ID" PARAMETERS '{"is_sequence": true}'
	COLUMN "SALARY_ANONYMIZED" PARAMETERS '{"is_sensitive":true, "epsilon":0.1, "sensitivity":2000}'
);
REFRESH VIEW "V_SALARY_SALARY_ANONYMIZED" ANONYMIZATION;

The V_SALARY_SALARY_ANONYMIZED view can now be imported into Datasphere (see 1.2.4 - Import the view into Datasphere)

2 - Standardization

We've seen above that Open SQL Schema is powerful to create features not implemented in the Datasphere UI but accessible using SQL.

The standardization will allow the SAP Datasphere users to easily activate/deactivate masking, scrambling and anonymization for columns.
A Datasphere local table (VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS) will contain the views definitions and a custom SQL procedure (EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR) will read its content to create the views based on the stored definitions. Then a Task Chain will be created to call the procedure from Datasphere.

2.1 - Parameters table

The table "VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS" will have the following columns:
  • "TARGET_VIEW": name of the view that will be created in the Open SQL Schema
  • "CONFIGURATION": characteristic of the target view. Contains the field name of the unmasked, scrambled anonymized field
  • "SEQ": optional sequence number used to order the list of the view fields
  • "VALUE": value related to the "CONFIGURATION". When "CONFIGURATION" is a field name, it contains an expression (or NULL when the field doesn't need to be changed in the target view) 
  • "CREATE": 'YES' when the target field needs to be created in the target table
  • "RENAME_TO": target field name when "CONFIGURATION" field name needs to be changed
  • "ACTIVATE": 'YES' when the target field "VALUE" needs to be used (when it doesn't start with the keyword 'PARAMETERS'). Otherwise the field name stored in "CONFIGURATION" is used.
CONFIGURATION contains one of the following keywords:
  • '#EXECUTE#': define the behavior of the procedure that will create the target view. When "VALUE" contains the value:
    • 'YES': it creates in the "TARGET_VIEW" view the fields with the 'CREATE' value set to 'YES'
    • 'ALL': it ignores the value of the "ACTIVATE" field ; all the fields with the 'CREATE' value set to 'YES' will be created with the expression stored in "CONFIGURATION"
    • 'RESET': if ignore the value of the "ACTIVATE" field ; all the fields with the 'CREATE' value set to 'YES' will be created with their original definition stored in "VALUE"
  • '#SOURCE_NAME#': when used, then "VALUE" contains the name of the table/view used to create the target view
  • '#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#': when used, then VALUE contains the expression used for anonymization (see SAP HANA Data Anonymization Guide)

 

TARGET_VIEWCONFIGURATIONSEQVALUECREATERENAME_TOACTIVATE
V_SALARY_DERIVED#EXECUTE# ALL   
V_SALARY_DERIVED#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE# WITH ANONYMIZATION (ALGORITHM 'DIFFERENTIAL_PRIVACY' PARAMETERS '{"data_change_strategy": "qualified"}'   
V_SALARY_DERIVED#SOURCE_NAME# V_SALARY   
V_SALARY_DERIVEDID1PARAMETERS '{"is_sequence": true}'YES YES
V_SALARY_DERIVEDNAME2INITCAP(SCRAMBLE_STRING(NAME))YESNAME_SCRAMBLEDYES
V_SALARY_DERIVEDGENDER3MAP(GENDER,'M','Mr','F','Ms','')YES  
V_SALARY_DERIVEDCITY4CITY  YES
V_SALARY_DERIVEDSALARY5PARAMETERS '{"is_sensitive":true, "epsilon":0.1, "sensitivity":2000}'YESSALARY_ANONYMIZEDYES
V_SALARY_DERIVEDCREDIT_CARD6LEFT(CREDIT_CARD,4) || '-XXXX-XXXX-' || RIGHT(CREDIT_CARD,4)YESCREDIT_CARD_MASKEDYES

In the previous screenshot, the view V_SALARY_DERIVED will have the following fields and properties:

  • "ID" will remain unchanged
  • "NAME" will be scrambled and renamed to "NAME_SCRAMBLED"
  • "GENDER" will appear not changed in the target view
  • "CITY" will not appear in the target view
  • "SALARY" will be anonymized and renamed to "SALARY_ANONYMIZED"
  • "CREDIT_CARD" will be masked and renamed to "CREDIT_CARD_MASKED"

In order to execute the expressions to create the target columns, a line with '#EXECUTE#' was created.
Since the view contains anonymized data, a line with '#ANONYZATION_TYPE#' was s created.

In order to be able to use this table content into an Open SQL Schema, an SQL view in SAP Datasphere needs to be created : V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS. This view will contain all the fields from the table.

 

2.2 - SQL Procedures

Three SQL procedures are created:

  • EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT: generates a create view statement 
  • EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR: with a string parameter used to filter the view V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS to create only one target view by calling the procedure EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT and embedding the code with instructions to drop the view if it already exists and generates the REFRESH statement if anonymization is used.
  • EXEC_ALL_VIEWS_CREATOR: calls EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR for the distinct values of TARGET_VEWS.

2.2.1 - Procedure  EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT

This procedure returns the CREATE VIEW statement for one target view defined in the view "VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS" .

In the code below:

  • replace DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE with the name of your Open SQL Schema
  • replace DEMO with your Datasphere space name.
CREATE PROCEDURE EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT (
	IN TARGET_NAME_ VARCHAR(1000),
	IN CURRENT_SPACE_ VARCHAR(1000),
	IN CURRENT_SCHEMA_ VARCHAR(1000),
	IN EXECUTE_ VARCHAR(1000),
	OUT FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ INTEGER,
	OUT SQL_DDL VARCHAR (2500)
)
AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE PART1 VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE PART2 VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE PART3 VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE PART4 VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE PART5 VARCHAR(2500);

	/*
	CREATE VIEW PART 1: begining part to create the target view
	*/
    SELECT TOP 1 'CREATE VIEW '||:CURRENT_SCHEMA_||'.'|| :TARGET_NAME_ AS x
    into PART1 
    FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS 
    WHERE "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;

	/*
	CREATE VIEW PART 2: list columns for the target view
	*/
	WITH VIEW_PARAMETERS as
		(SELECT
			"TARGET_VIEW" AS "TARGET_VIEW",
			"CONFIGURATION" AS "CONFIGURATION",
			"SEQ" AS "SEQ",
			CASE :EXECUTE_ 
				WHEN 'YES' 
					THEN CASE WHEN IFNULL("ACTIVATE", 'NO')<>'YES'
						THEN "CONFIGURATION"
						ELSE "VALUE"
					END
				WHEN 'ALL' THEN "VALUE"
				WHEN 'RESET' THEN "CONFIGURATION" 
			END AS "VALUE",
			"CREATE" AS "CREATE",
			"RENAME_TO" AS "RENAME_TO"
		FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS
		WHERE "CONFIGURATION" NOT IN ('#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#', '#EXECUTE#', '#SOURCE_NAME#')
			AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_
		)
    SELECT ' ( ' ||
	    string_agg( IFNULL(RENAME_TO,"CONFIGURATION" )
	    	    , ', ' ORDER BY "SEQ" ) ||
	    ' ) as select ' ||
	    string_agg( 
	        IFNULL(
	            ( CASE WHEN "VALUE" LIKE 'PARAMETERS%' 
					THEN NULL 
					ELSE "VALUE" 
	            END ) 
	            , "CONFIGURATION"
	        ) ||
			' as ' ||
			IFNULL( RENAME_TO, "CONFIGURATION" )
			, ', ' ORDER BY "SEQ"
		) 
    AS x 
    INTO PART2
    FROM VIEW_PARAMETERS
    WHERE "CREATE" = 'YES';
    
	/*
	CREATE VIEW PART 3: select expression for the target view
	*/
	SELECT TOP 1 ' from '|| :CURRENT_SPACE_ || '.' || "VALUE" 
	as x 
	into PART3 
	FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS 
	WHERE "CONFIGURATION" = '#SOURCE_NAME#' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;

	WITH VIEW_PARAMETERS as
		(SELECT
			"TARGET_VIEW" AS "TARGET_VIEW",
			CASE :EXECUTE_ 
				WHEN 'YES' 
					THEN CASE WHEN IFNULL("ACTIVATE", 'NO')<>'YES'
						THEN "CONFIGURATION"
						ELSE "VALUE"
					END
				WHEN 'ALL' THEN "VALUE"
				WHEN 'RESET' THEN "CONFIGURATION" 
			END AS "VALUE"
		FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS
		WHERE "CONFIGURATION" NOT IN ('#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#', '#EXECUTE#', '#SOURCE_NAME#')
			AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_ AND "CREATE"='YES'
			AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 
						FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS 
						WHERE UPPER("CONFIGURATION") = '#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_ AND :EXECUTE_ in ('YES','ALL')
						)
		)
	SELECT count(*) as x
	INTO FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_
	FROM VIEW_PARAMETERS
	WHERE "VALUE" LIKE 'PARAMETERS%' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;
	
	IF :FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ <> 0 THEN
		/*
		CREATE VIEW PART 4: add predicate for anonymization
		*/
	    SELECT "VALUE" AS x 
	    INTO PART4 
	    FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS 
	    WHERE "CONFIGURATION" = '#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;
	
		/*
		CREATE VIEW PART 5: list parameters for anonymization
		*/
		WITH VIEW_PARAMETERS as
		(SELECT
			"TARGET_VIEW" AS "TARGET_VIEW",
			"CONFIGURATION" AS "CONFIGURATION",
			"SEQ" AS "SEQ",
			CASE :EXECUTE_ 
				WHEN 'YES' 
					THEN CASE WHEN IFNULL("ACTIVATE", 'NO')<>'YES'
						THEN "CONFIGURATION"
						ELSE "VALUE"
					END
				WHEN 'ALL' THEN "VALUE"
				WHEN 'RESET' THEN "CONFIGURATION" 
			END AS "VALUE",
			"RENAME_TO" as "RENAME_TO"
		FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS
		WHERE "CONFIGURATION" NOT IN ('#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#', '#EXECUTE#', '#SOURCE_NAME#')
			AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_ AND "CREATE"='YES'
			AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 
						FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS 
						WHERE UPPER("CONFIGURATION") = '#ANONYMIZATION_TYPE#' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_ AND :EXECUTE_ in ('YES','ALL')
						)
		)
		SELECT string_agg('Column ' || IFNULL(RENAME_TO,"CONFIGURATION") || ' ' || "VALUE", ' ' ORDER BY "SEQ") ||' )' AS x
	    INTO PART5
	    FROM VIEW_PARAMETERS
	    WHERE UPPER(LEFT("VALUE",10))='PARAMETERS' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;
		
	END IF;	   
	
    select string_agg(x, ' ' order by rn) as x 
    into SQL_DDL from (
	    SELECT :PART1 as x ,1 as rn from DUMMY UNION ALL
	    SELECT :PART2 as x ,2 as rn from DUMMY UNION ALL
	    SELECT :PART3 as x ,3 as rn from DUMMY UNION ALL
	    SELECT :PART4 as x ,4 as rn from DUMMY UNION ALL    
	    SELECT :PART5 as x ,5 as rn from DUMMY
    ) a ;
END

 

2.2.2 - Procedure  EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR

This procedure creates the SQL instructions to create one target view.

It contains:

  • a DROP command that will be executed if the TARGET_VIEW is already existing
  • a CREATE VIEW command to create the TARGET_VIEW
  • A REFRESH command if TARGET_VIEW contains any field that needs to be anonymized

In the code below:

  • replace DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE with the name of your Open SQL Schema
  • replace DEMO with your Datasphere space name.
CREATE PROCEDURE EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR (IN TARGET_NAME_ VARCHAR (1000)) 
AS
BEGIN
    DECLARE SQL_DROP VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE SQL_DDL VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE EXECUTE_ VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE REFRESH_ VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE FLAG_DROP_ INTEGER;
    DECLARE FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ INTEGER;
    DECLARE CURRENT_SCHEMA_ VARCHAR(2500);
    DECLARE CURRENT_SPACE_ VARCHAR(2500);

SELECT UPPER("VALUE") as x into EXECUTE_ FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS WHERE UPPER("CONFIGURATION") = '#EXECUTE#' AND "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;

IF :EXECUTE_ IN ('YES', 'RESET', 'ALL') THEN
	SELECT 'DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE' into CURRENT_SCHEMA_ FROM DUMMY;
	SELECT SUBSTR_BEFORE(:CURRENT_SCHEMA_, '#') into CURRENT_SPACE_ from DUMMY;
    SELECT count (*) INTO FLAG_DROP_ FROM views WHERE schema_name=:CURRENT_SCHEMA_ and view_name=:TARGET_NAME_;
   
    /*
    DROP SQL expression to premare the replacement for the target view.
    */
    SELECT TOP 1 'DROP VIEW '||:CURRENT_SCHEMA_||'.'||:TARGET_NAME_ ||' ; ' as x 
    into SQL_DROP
    from DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS WHERE "TARGET_VIEW"=:TARGET_NAME_;

    /*
    CREATE SQL expression to create the target view.
    */
	CALL "DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE"."EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR_CREATE_STATMT"(
		TARGET_NAME_ => :TARGET_NAME_ /*<NVARCHAR(1000)>*/,
		CURRENT_SPACE_ => :CURRENT_SPACE_ /*<NVARCHAR(1000)>*/,
		CURRENT_SCHEMA_ => :CURRENT_SCHEMA_ /*<NVARCHAR(1000)>*/,
		EXECUTE_ => :EXECUTE_/*<NVARCHAR(1000)>*/,
		FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ => FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_,
		SQL_DDL => SQL_DDL
	);
	
	IF :FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ <> 0 THEN    
	    /*
	    REFRESH SQL expression for anonymization feature.
	    */
	    SELECT 'REFRESH VIEW '|| :CURRENT_SCHEMA_ ||'.'|| :TARGET_NAME_ || ' ANONYMIZATION;'
	    INTO REFRESH_ 
	    FROM DUMMY ;
	END IF;

    IF FLAG_DROP_ <> 0 THEN 
        EXECUTE IMMEDIATE SQL_DROP;
    END IF;

    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE SQL_DDL;
    IF :FLAG_ANONYMIZATION_ <> 0 THEN
    	EXECUTE IMMEDIATE REFRESH_;
    END IF;

END IF;
END

2.2.3 - Procedure  EXEC_ALL_VIEWS_CREATOR

This procedure calls the procedure EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR for all the distinct values for TARGET_VIEW.

In the code below:

  • replace DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE with the name of your Open SQL Schema
  • replace DEMO with your Datasphere space name.
CREATE PROCEDURE EXEC_ALL_VIEWS_CREATOR()
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
AS
BEGIN
	DECLARE CURSOR c_cursor FOR SELECT DISTINCT TARGET_VIEW FROM DEMO.V_VIEWS_CREATOR_PARAMETERS;
	FOR rec as c_cursor
	DO
	    CALL "DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE"."EXEC_VIEWS_CREATOR" (:rec.TARGET_VIEW);
  END FOR;
END

2.3 - Task Chain

In order to be autonomous to mask, scramble and anonymize data in Datasphere (without accessing to the Open SQL Schema) a task chain can be created to call the SQL procedure EXEC_ALL_VIEWS_CREATOR.

2.3.1 - Grant permissions

Datasphere space needs to view and run the SQL Procedure that was created in the Open SQL Schema, some grants need to be done in SQL Developer by  the user DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE to the Datasphere space user.

In the code below:

  • replace DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE with the name of your Open SQL Schema
  • replace DEMO with your Datasphere space name.
 
CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE" (
	OPERATION => 'GRANT', 
	PRIVILEGE => 'EXECUTE', 
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DEMO#USER_ANONYMIZE', 
	OBJECT_NAME => '', 
	SPACE_ID => 'DEMO');

2.3.2 - Create a task chain

 

2.3.3 - Test

For testing purposes, we can use the parameters table described in 2.1 - Parameters table .

After running the task chain, the view V_SALARY_DERIVED was created and can now be imported into the Datasphere space (see 1.2.4 - Import the view into Datasphere ).

Finally your space should contain this list of objects: 

 

If you open V_SALARY_DERIVED:

 

Conclusion

If you face some missing features in Datasphere, consider Open SQL Schema, it may offer you the opportunity to implement what you miss using the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database.

If you liked this blog, say it by clicking on the Like button.

entry.title: Rewiring of SAP Datasphere to SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
The rewiring of existing SAP Datasphere tenants to SAP Business Data Cloud has become available in 2025. Since then, I have been in many discussions with customers, partners, but also SAP colleagues at SAP TechEd in Berlin, Sydney and other events or meetings.

I observed that people unintentionally misinterpret the term “rewiring”. It sounds technical, hence there are associations with data, object or even full tenant migrations. This is not the case!

Let me use this blog to clarify what rewiring is, and what it is not. Moreover, give you some guidance on the steps which you need to take.

 

 

When do I need to rewire my existing SAP Datasphere tenant?

You have made a decision to sign up for SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and to make use of your existing tenants for SAP Datasphere and / or SAP Analytics Cloud as part of your new landscape with SAP BDC.

If you have not made any decision on SAP BDC yet, you can certainly use your existing tenant(s) until the end of your current contract without SAP BDC. However, you will miss out on new capabilities offered by SAP BDC with SAP managed data products and the enhanced open partner ecosystem options.

 

What is tenant rewiring?

The term “rewiring” sounds quite technical. Therefore, there are immediate associations with data, object or even full tenant migrations. This is not the case!

  1. The term rewiring mainly refers to a change of your commercial model from a stand-alone tenant to a tenant which is managed as part of a larger SAP BDC landscape.
  2. Rewiring helps you change the management of your tenant lifecycle to the SAP for Me portal.
  3. Prerequisites are that you commercially move the tenant on your same customer ID and your current data center being available also for Business Data Cloud. Please check on these two points!
  4. If these conditions are met a lot will stay the same. Meaning you stay on your existing hyperscaler and data center. Hence, your tenant and its URL will remain intact, just under a new commercial model, even without any downtime required.

 

What is tenant rewiring not?

Rewiring is therefore not a tenant migration as your existing tenant stays the same and is reused as part of an SAP BDC landscape.

Rewiring does not migrate your tenant between hyperscalers or data centers, as with the conditions mentioned before, your current data center – given that it is available for SAP BDC – will stay the same on your existing customer ID.

Rewiring does not migrate your data or any objects, as the full existing tenant is only commercially moved as described before.

Rewiring does not require any tenant downtime.

 

How can I initiate a tenant rewiring?

Before you can start to initiate the rewiring, you need to change the commercial model of your SAP Datasphere tenant to a Business Data Cloud contract – in short: a subscription to SAP Business Data Cloud must exist.

For tenants provisioned via BTP cockpit

After the commercial change, your SAP BTP administrator can initiate the tenant rewiring directly through the SAP BTP platform global account as a self-service. You control the timing of rewiring yourself, as you can trigger it via self-service.

Once the request is received, the process is initiated to update the tenant and move it to SAP Business Data Cloud. The tenant will be visible in SAP Business Data Cloud when the rewiring is completed.

For subscription tenants provisioned by SAP

After the commercial change, your subscription is moved from standalone to SAP Business Data Cloud. Be aware, that the SAP Business Data Cloud cockpit feature must be activated in your appropriate data center through SAP for Me portal prior to rewiring.

The rewiring itself is initiated as part of the subscription change request. That means, your tenant is automatically moved to SAP Business Data Cloud for the contract start date.

 

Where do I find more detailed information?

For more details I recommend these three resources:

SAP Community Blog by @Ammar_Naji:
🔗 UPDATE: SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud Availability via SAP Business Data Cloud

SAP Help documentation for detailed steps:
🔗  Rewiring of Standalone SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud Subscriptions to SAP Business Data Cloud

🔗  SAP Note 3650483

 

Let me know if there are still questions about rewiring and post them below 👇 in the comments section. I will try to answer them and loop in colleagues in case I do not know the answer myself.

entry.title: Authorization setup for SAC Import Model from S4 ABAP CDS Views
entry.content:
Purpose of this document

The purpose of this blog is to explain how to implement data-level authorization in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories when:

  • Data is extracted from SAP S/4HANA CDS Views
  • The connection type is Import Model
  • Authorization must be enforced based on Company Code and Plant

This approach ensures that users can access only the data they are authorized to see, even though the data is physically stored in SAC.

 

Business Use Case

In many finance and controlling scenarios:

  • A single SAC Story is shared across multiple business users
  • Users belong to different Company Codes and Plant
  • Data is periodically imported from SAP S/4HANA into SAC

Since Import Models store data in SAC, runtime authorisation from S/4HANA (CDS DCLs) is not applied. Therefore, authorization must be designed and enforced within SAC.

 

Authorization Concept for Import Models

When working with SAC Import Models, authorization is handled as follows:

Layer

Responsibility

SAP S/4HANA CDS

Defines which data is extracted

SAC Model

Enforces row-level data authorization

SAC Story

Automatically respects model-level security

Key points to note:
  • CDS DCLs are evaluated only at data extraction time
  • SAC Data Access Control (DAC) governs user visibility
  • Story designers do not need to manage authorization logic
Example Used in This Blog

The same example is used consistently throughout the blog.

Attribute

Value

Authorization Dimensions

Company Code, Plant

Sample Company Code

0100

Sample Plant

RA01, RA02, …

SAC Connection Type

Import

Source System

SAP S/4HANA CDS View

 

Step-by-Step Authorization Setup

Step 1: Prepare CDS View in SAP S/4HANA

Ensure that the CDS View contains the fields required for authorization, i.e., Company Code and Plant.

Example CDS Used:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDEMO002V'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Authorization Consumption VIew'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Analytics.query: true
@VDM.viewType: #CONSUMPTION
define view ZC_DEMO_AUTH_2
  as select from ZI_DEMO_AUTH_1
{
  PurchaseOrder,
  PurchaseOrderItem,
  PurchaseOrderCategory,
  CompanyCode, // Authorization field
  Plant,       // Authorization Field
  @Semantics.currencyCode: true
  DocumentCurrency,
  @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'DocumentCurrency'
  PurOrderNetAmount
}

Important notes:

  • Authorization-relevant fields must be part of the CDS output
  • CDS DCLs are not used for runtime authorization in SAC Import Models

 

Step 2: Create Import Model in SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Navigate to Connections in SAC.
  • Create an SAP S/4HANA Import Connection.
  • Import the CDS View as a Model.

  • At this stage, the data is physically stored in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Step 3: Enable Data Access Control (DAC)

  • Open the imported Model
  • Navigate to Settings
  • Navigate to Model Preferences
  • Enable Data Access Control

  • This step activates row-level authorization for the model.

 

Step 4: Mark Authorization-Relevant Dimensions

  • Enable data access control for the required dimensions.
  • Select CompanyCode, and Plant.

  • These dimensions are now evaluated during data access.

 

Step 5: Create Data Access Roles

  • Navigate to Security → Data Access
  • Create a new Data Access Role

  • Provide Role information, Role template, and review before creating. Template helps to create roles fast for general sections like BI Admin or Planner. In this we will choose template as ‘Blank’.

  • After role is created, we will move to ‘Select Model’, choose your import model.

  • Select dimensions required for authorizations and provide values for each.

 

Step 6: Assign Data Access Role to Users

  • Navigate to Security → Roles
  • Select the relevant user
  • Assign Business Role – SAC_ROLE_DEMO_001 to users for Row-level authorization.
  • The user now inherits Company Code and Plant restrictions.

 

Step 7: Validate Authorization in SAC Story

  • Create or open a Story based on the model.
  • Add a table or chart in story for validation.
  • Include Company Code and Plant as dimensions.
  • Authorization is automatically enforced at runtime.

 

Results

After completing the setup:

  • Data visibility is restricted at row level.
  • A single Story can be reused for multiple user groups.
  • No duplication of Stories or Models is required.
  • Authorization is centrally managed in SAC.
  • Import performance remains unchanged.

 

Best Practices

  • Enable Data Access Control immediately after model creation.
  • Use consistent authorization dimensions across models.
  • Follow clear and business-aligned naming conventions for roles.
  • Do not rely on CDS DCLs for Import Model security.
  • Always validate authorization using multiple test users.

 

Conclusion

When working with SAP Analytics Cloud Import Models based on SAP S/4HANA CDS Views, authorization must be implemented within SAC.

By using:

  • Data Access Control
  • Authorization-enabled dimensions
  • Data Access Roles

you can achieve secure, scalable, and reusable Story-level authorization based on Company Code and Plant fully aligned with standard SAP practices.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in December
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere News in December

What a year it has been. Wow! 2025 was packed with great events, lots of new features and enhancements to extend existing features, and the launch of SAP Business Data Cloud. 
Explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and enjoy watching my top feature highlights for 2025 on YouTube 📺 below.

 

SAP Datasphere 2025 highlights in a nutshell

What a great ride it has been in 2025 with SAP Datasphere!
Let me share a few of my personal highlights.

SAP Business Data Cloud

The announcement of SAP Business Data Cloud in February this year made quite some noise and puts data, analytics and AI at the center of our portfolio at SAP. We delivered SAP Business Data Cloud in May 2025 as a fully managed software as a service solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data.

It is an evolution of the business data fabric journey we started with SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud some years ago and also offers a path forward for your investments in SAP BW.

Delivering out-of-the-box always-on data products is the next pivotal step in our data strategy following a harmonized data model to provide scalable access without duplication using zero-copy delta share technology.

SAP BDC serves as the foundation for your data and AI projects leveraging SAP managed and custom data products, including our growing open partner ecosystem with Databricks and the newly announced partnerships with Google Big Query, Snowflake and Microsoft Fabric.

 

My top features in 2025

Picking my top features for all of 2025 was not easy and here is my top feature list in no particular order.

 

Object store (general availability)

In 2025 we delivered the option to include an object store next to the well-known SAP HANA Cloud based persistency for a cost-efficient storage. You can use the existing tooling you already know to now create local tables on files, use replication flows, transformation flows which are then using Spark as compute engine - all orchestrated by task chains. You can load large quantities of data via replication flows and prepare the data using transformation flows and then share data to other spaces to be used as a source for additional flows, views, and of course analytic models. A really great enhancement!

 

Versioning of data builder objects

Object versioning has been introduced for views, analytic models, data access controls, and local tables. The new object versions are created automatically at the time of deployment which allows you now to list the past versions of objects, open a past version in read-only mode in a new tab, download a past version to a CSN/JSON file and restore a past version to replace the current version.

Be aware that the saving of an error-free restored version overwrites the current object. It is also important to understand that the versioning happens for the metadata of an object and not the data.

 

Efficiency features

Then there are tons of smaller enhancements that have been delivered in 2025 to increase efficiency and productivity overall. Let me just name a few here for the different components of Datasphere.

Analytic model

  •  stacking and reusing of models
  •  support for structures and data access controls for analytic models
  •  we introduced a measure-dependency graph, the fiscal time dimension and unit conversion
  •  and many enhancements with variables and parameters

Replication flows

  • delta only load type
  • support for views
  • reusing of source objects in multiple flows
  • many new sources and targets

Transformation flows

  • batch processing
  • parameter support
  • HANA tables as source in Spark transformation flows
  • other features bringing Spark transformation flows almost on par with HANA Cloud based ones

Task chains

  • deep retry of failed tasks in nested task chains
  • Rest API
  • Parameter support
  • notification tasks
  • amongst other improvements

Catalog

  • Support for SAP BW/4HANA, seamless planning, SAP BDC data products, Databricks via BDC connect
  • GenAI-assisted generation of catalog asset descriptions and classifications
  • Mass import of existing glossaries, terms, KPI’s, etc. from flat files

 

SAP Cloud application lifecycle management

SAP Datasphere is now integrated into SAP Cloud application lifecycle management (ALM) for health, job and automation monitoring.

Health monitoring enables you to check the health of one or more Datasphere tenants from the Health Monitoring app in Cloud ALM to check on 

  • Memory and Disk usage
  • Out of Memory Events
  • Task Failures and
  • Admission Control Events

Job and automation monitoring enables you to monitor the tasks runs and executions in one or more SAP Datasphere tenants from the Job and Automation Monitoring app in Cloud ALM.

The integration overall eases the monitoring of an SAP Datasphere tenant in a complex customer landscape with a centralized, consistent way of tracking the tenants.

In addition, the early watch alert reports are now also available for SAP Datasphere. EWA is an automatic service analyzing the essential administrative areas of your SAP systems and covered by your maintenance agreement.

 

Data access controls based on IdP attributes

Data access controls based on identity provider (IdP) attributes are a small enhancement, but it is very powerful. You can now use values in custom attributes provided by your IdP as identifiers in your data access controls and define conditions that apply to users with these values. It is powerful because using these attributes allows you to apply a single condition to all users whose accounts contain that specific attribute value. In other words, you can group users with attributes to assign permissions to a group instead of individual users.

 

YouTube feature highlights of 2025

 

 

 

And more feature highlights of 2025

There are so many more great features which we delivered in 2025, so in addition to my 2025 top features, I pulled together the 2025 feature highlights in all different areas of SAP Datasphere for you. Enjoy the ride!

Data Integration

 

 

Catalog & Monitoring

 

Data Modelling

 

Miscellaneous

 

What exactly is rewiring for SAP Datasphere?

I have heard that question so many times over the last few months from customers, partners, but also SAP colleagues at events like SAP TechEd in Berlin or Sydney. 
During these discussions I observed that people unintentionally misinterpret the term “rewiring”. It sounds technical, hence there are associations with data, object or even full tenant migrations. This is not the case!

Therefore, I wrote a blog to clarify what rewiring is, and what it is not. Moreover, give you some guidance on the steps which you need to take. 
🔗   https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/rewiring-of-sap-datasphere-to-sap-business-data-cloud/ba-p/14292149

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • Connecting SAP Analytics Cloud to Databricks model serving endpoint
  • SAP for Me – Adoption Report – Drive More Business Value Realization
  • SAP Analytics Cloud ライセンス説明およびシミュレーション機能
  • Partner learning accelerator: Certified academy for SAP Business Data Cloud
  • RANDOM FOREST ITERATION 1
  • Leverage data products in the context of planning & forecasting
  • What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud – December 2025
  • SAP BW Modernization: A Smarter Path to a Future-Ready Data Foundation
  • Rewiring of SAP Datasphere to SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Data masking, data scrambling and data anonymization in Business Data Cloud with SAP Datasphere

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting. Also check out the new support content for SAP Datasphere on SAP Help for troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: Working with BW Authorizations in Datasphere on Enterprise Level
entry.content:
Related resources

  • Blog Post: Introducing Remote Authorizations from SAP BW/4HANA for SAP Datasphere 
  • Blog Post by @Martin_Kuma: BW-Like Authorizations in Datasphere (DSP) 
  • Documentation: Import SAP BW and SAP BW∕4HANA Analysis Authorizations 
  • Note: 3062381

 

Why resolve BW authorizations in Datasphere?

In enterprise analytics SAP Datasphere often needs to federates the SAP BW analysis authorization table RSDWC_RSEC_DAC to data access controls (DAC). We build on this SAP standard (no custom BW developments are required beyond generation/extraction of the standard table) and bring the resolution step into the HANA layer for scale and performance.

Performance matters: The default runtime-only resolution evaluates long WHERE predicate strings per session user and iterates them at query time. With many users and complex rules, that becomes inperformant and unpredictable in terms of latency. By pre‑resolving per objectinto a persisted table we decouple expensive checks from BI queries, achieve consistent, auditable results, and enable scheduled refreshes.

 

Prerequisites

  1. RSDWC_RSEC_DAC as a Remote Table in Datasphere. Generate/refresh it in BW with transaction RSDWC_DAC_RSEC_GEN and the BAdI RSDWC_DAC_RSEC_USER_UPDATE (and optionally schedule report RSDWC_DAC_RSEC_EXTRACT). The table can then be exposed to Datasphere. 
  2. A HANA DB user (Open SQL schema) in Datasphere. This user owns the Function, Procedure and Table and is granted to the space technical user to run in Task Chains.
  3. Optional: an Entities/"booked values" table/view. Applying predicates to the set of actually booked combinations avoids generating all possible cross‑combinations which can be a huge performance multiplier and inflate result sizes.

 

Where our default approaches falls short

  • Runtime resolution per current user leads to high CPU/time due to long predicate strings.
  • SQL views in Datasphere lack loops/cursors; scaling to many users is cumbersome.
  • Duplicating views per user and UNION them is not maintainable and limits parallelization.

 

Example at a glance

In this blog’s walkthrough OBJECT01 is our authorization object that groups the key BW characteristics used for data access in the BWAUTH02 mapping. Concretely, OBJECT01 controls combinations of the following standard InfoObjects:

  • /BIC/0COMP_CODE (Company Code)
  • /BIC/0PLANT (Plant)
  • /BIC/0SALESORG (Sales Organization)
  • /BIC/0PROFIT_CTR (Profit Center)
  • /BIC/0CS_PLEVEL

The SAP standard table RSDWC_RSEC_DAC (federated as a remote table to Datasphere) holds per BW user and per object the dynamic WHERE‑predicate string that expresses allowed values/ranges for these characteristics.

 

Solution overview: Function + Procedure + Table + Task Chain + View

We implement five building blocks in an Open SQL schema and orchestrate them from Datasphere:

  1. Table Function `TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02`: reads normalized WHERE predicate from RSEC view and applies it to booked entities via `APPLY_FILTER`.
  2. Procedure `SP_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02`: loops over all BW users for OBJECT01 and persists the union of results into a target table (full refresh).
  3. Persisted table `AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02`: mirrors function output for /BIC/0* fields.
  4. View `GV_AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02`: exposes the table for DACs and downstream pipelines.
  5. Task Chain `TC_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02`: schedules the refresh procedure regularly.

Source artifacts and naming

  • RSEC (clean) view: "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_RSDWC_RSEC_DAC_BWAUTH02" (column FILTERFIELDNM holds the normalized predicate)
  • Entities view (booked combinations): "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_OBJECT01_DAC_ENTITIES_BOOKED_BWAUTH02"
  • Target table: "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02"
  • Exposing view: "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02"
  • Task Chain: TC_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02

Target table 

CREATE COLUMN TABLE "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02" (
    "User"             NVARCHAR(256),
    "OBJECTNAME"       NVARCHAR(40),
    "/BIC/0PLANT"      NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0SALESORG"   NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL"  NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR" NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0COMP_CODE"  NVARCHAR(20)
);

Table Function 

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   Object:   FUNCTION "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02"
   Purpose:  For a given BW user, returns the authorized ENTITY combinations
             for OBJECT01 (fixed here; can be parameterized later).
             The WHERE predicate string is read dynamically from the RSEC view
             and applied to the Entities view via APPLY_FILTER.

   Input:    P_USER (NVARCHAR(256)) – BWUSERID for which the authorization resolution is executed.

   Output:   Result set with the following columns (aligned to the new standard InfoObjects):
             "User", "OBJECTNAME",
             "/BIC/0PLANT", "/BIC/0SALESORG", "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL",
             "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR", "/BIC/0COMP_CODE"

   Assumptions & behavior:
     - Exactly ONE record exists in the RSEC view for (BWUSERID = P_USER, OBJECTNAME = 'OBJECT01_BWAUTH02').
       -> SELECT ... INTO expects exactly 1 row. More than one -> "row not unique"; none -> "no data found".
     - INFOOBJ is intentionally fixed to 'OBJECT01_BWAUTH02' for this scenario.
     - APPLY_FILTER expects a syntactically valid WHERE string (without the "WHERE" keyword).

   Security/runtime:
     - SQL SECURITY INVOKER: Privilege checks are executed in the caller's context.
       -> Caller must have SELECT on the views used in the space/schema.
     - APPLY_FILTER is safer than EXEC/EXECUTE IMMEDIATE regarding SQL injection,
       however FILTERFIELDNM must be maintained reliably in the RSEC view.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------*/
CREATE FUNCTION "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02"(
    IN P_USER NVARCHAR(256)
)
RETURNS TABLE (
    "User"           NVARCHAR(256),
    "OBJECTNAME"     NVARCHAR(40),
    "/BIC/0PLANT"    NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0SALESORG" NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL" NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR" NVARCHAR(20),
    "/BIC/0COMP_CODE"  NVARCHAR(20)
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY INVOKER
AS
BEGIN
    /* Local variables:
       - FILTER_CLAUSE: holds the dynamic WHERE predicate read from the RSEC view
       - INFOOBJ: fixed to OBJECT01 (current scenario)
    */
    DECLARE FILTER_CLAUSE NCLOB;
    DECLARE INFOOBJ NVARCHAR(40);

    /* Fixed authorization object; can be modeled as IN parameter if needed */
    INFOOBJ = 'OBJECT01';

    /* 1) Load filter string for the user.
          Expects exactly one match. If SELECT returns 0 or >1 rows, HANA raises an exception.
          This should be ensured in the RSEC view content. */
    SELECT FILTERFIELDNM
      INTO FILTER_CLAUSE
      FROM "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_RSDWC_RSEC_DAC_BWAUTH02"
     WHERE BWUSERID   = :P_USER
       AND OBJECTNAME = INFOOBJ;

    /* 2) APPLY_FILTER:
          - Applies the dynamic WHERE expression to the entities view.
          - The result is materialized in the implicit table variable lt_result. */
    lt_result = APPLY_FILTER(
        "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_OBJECT01_DAC_ENTITIES_BOOKED_BWAUTH02",
        :FILTER_CLAUSE
    );

    /* 3) Assemble result:
          - DISTINCT -> avoid duplicates.
          - P_USER and INFOOBJ are carried as constant columns. */
    RETURN
    SELECT DISTINCT
        :P_USER    AS "User",
        INFOOBJ    AS "OBJECTNAME",
        "/BIC/0PLANT",
        "/BIC/0SALESORG",
        "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL",
        "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR",
        "/BIC/0COMP_CODE"
    FROM :lt_result;
END;

You can check how it is working: 

SELECT * FROM "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02"('ABC02')

Procedure

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   Object:   PROCEDURE "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."SP_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02"
   Purpose:  Full-refresh build of table "AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02":
             - Collect all BWUSERID relevant for OBJECT01 (from GV_RSDWC_RSEC_DAC_BWAUTH02)
             - For each user, call table function TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02
             - Persist the union of results into the target table

   Flow:
     1) Cursor over DISTINCT BWUSERID from the RSEC view (OBJECTNAME = 'OBJECT01')
     2) DELETE the target table (clear all rows for a full rebuild)
     3) FOR-loop: INSERT ... SELECT from the table function for each user

   Security:
     - SQL SECURITY INVOKER: privilege checks run in caller context
     - Caller requires:
       * SELECT on source RSEC view
       * INSERT/DELETE on target table

   Performance:
     - Loop calls the TF once per user (N calls).
       For large N, consider a set-based approach (bulk table function) later.

   Notes:
     - INSERT lists columns explicitly to guard against future schema changes
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------*/
CREATE PROCEDURE "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."SP_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02" ()
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY INVOKER
AS
BEGIN

  /* 1) Cursor: distinct list of relevant users for OBJECT01 */
  DECLARE CURSOR cur_users FOR
    SELECT DISTINCT BWUSERID
      FROM "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_RSDWC_RSEC_DAC_BWAUTH02"
     WHERE OBJECTNAME = 'OBJECT01'
       AND BWUSERID   IS NOT NULL;

  /* 2) Full rebuild: clear target table */
  DELETE FROM "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02";

  /* 3) Implicit FOR-loop: HANA opens/closes the cursor automatically */
  FOR cur_row AS cur_users DO

    INSERT INTO "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02"
      ( "User", "OBJECTNAME",
        "/BIC/0PLANT", "/BIC/0SALESORG", "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL",
        "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR", "/BIC/0COMP_CODE" )
    SELECT
        "User", "OBJECTNAME",
        "/BIC/0PLANT", "/BIC/0SALESORG", "/BIC/0CS_PLEVEL",
        "/BIC/0PROFIT_CTR", "/BIC/0COMP_CODE"
    FROM "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."TF_AUTH_RESOLVE_OBJECT01_BWAUTH02"( :cur_row.BWUSERID );

  END FOR;

END;

Execute via Task Chain step or manually:

CALL "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."SP_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02"();
SELECT * FROM "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02";

Security, view exposure, and task chain

  • Create Datasphere view: "DEMO_AR_001"."GV_AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02" over the table to feed DACs and downstream processes. 

  • Schedule a Task Chain "TC_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02" that calls the procedure for regular refresh.

To add the procedure your user needs to be granted: 

-- Minimal grants (adapt to your technical user / space)
GRANT EXECUTE ON PROCEDURE "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."SP_AUTH_OBJECT01_REFRESH_BWAUTH02" TO "DEMO_AR_001";
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, DELETE ON "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01"."AUTH_OBJECT01_RESULT_BWAUTH02" TO "DEMO_AR_001";
-- Optional: schema-wide
GRANT EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DEMO_AR_001#HDB_USER01" TO "DEMO_AR_001" WITH GRANT OPTION;

If you do not know the needed Schema: 

SELECT SCHEMA_NAME, SCHEMA_OWNER
FROM SYS.SCHEMAS

 

Performance & scalability

  • Pre-resolving moves string evaluation out of query-time; persisted results enable indexes and faster joins.
  • Keep the entities view "booked" to avoid generating all combinations (Cartesian blow-up).
  • For very large user counts, consider a set-based bulk table function to avoid N function calls.

 

Troubleshooting

  • Error 260 invalid column name: fix duplicated /BIC/ prefixes or mismatched InfoObject names.
  • No data found / row not unique: enforce exactly 1 row per (BWUSERID, OBJECTNAME) in RSEC view.
  • Permissions: INVOKER needs SELECT on sources and INSERT/DELETE on target; scheduler user needs EXECUTE on procedure.

 

What you get

A maintainable, enterprise-ready pattern to pre-resolve BW authorizations in SAP Datasphere, fully based on SAP standard RSDWC_RSEC_DAC: reproducible results, faster queries and a scheduling hook via Task Chains. From here, you can attach DACs to exposed views or plug the table into harmonization and governance pipelines.

entry.title: High Impact SAP Data Objects to Clean for an S/4HANA Selective Transition from ECC
entry.content:
*Views Expressed here are my own and doesn't represent any entity.

Introduction

Moving from SAP ECC to S/4HANA using a Selective Data Transition is not just a technical system conversion,it is a data transformation journey. While selective carve outs allow organizations to migrate only relevant organizational units, time slices, or data sets, poor data quality can jeopardize the entire S/4HANA transformation.S/4HANA’s simplified data model, stricter checks, and real-time processing mean that legacy inconsistencies tolerated in ECC will cause errors, functional gaps, or performance issues in S/4HANA.

This blog outlines the SAP data objects that require mandatory data cleaning before a selective S/4HANA migration, along with practical examples and rationale for each.

Why Data Cleaning Is Mandatory in a Selective Transition

In a selective approach:

  • Only chosen company codes, plants, or years are migrated

  • Historical inconsistencies become more visible

  • Data is restructured, not simply converted

S/4HANA:

  • Enforces business partner (BP) as a single master

  • Removes many legacy tables

  • Uses real-time reconciliation

  • Requires consistent master and transactional data

Without cleaning, you risk:

  • Migration failures

  • Reconciliation mismatches

  • Broken business processes

  • Delayed go-lives

1. Business Partner (Customer & Vendor)

What Needs Cleaning

  • Duplicate customers/vendors

  • Inconsistent master data across company codes

  • Missing mandatory fields (address, tax, bank details)

  • Vendors acting as customers (and vice versa)

ECC Example

  • Vendor 100045 exists for AP

  • Customer 200078 exists for AR

  • Same legal entity, different tax IDs and addresses

Why This Is Mandatory

In S/4HANA:

  • Customers and Vendors are merged into Business Partner

  • Roles (Customer, Supplier) are assigned to a single BP number

  • Inconsistent data causes CVI (Customer Vendor Integration) errors

Rationale

Selective transitions often migrate only certain company codes, making duplicates harder to reconcile post-migration. Cleaning ensures:

  • Smooth BP conversion

  • Accurate AR/AP reporting

  • Compliance with S/4 data model

2. Material Master Data

What Needs Cleaning

  • Obsolete or unused materials

  • Incomplete views (MM, SD, PP)

  • Invalid units of measure

  • Duplicate material numbers

ECC Example

  • Material created but never used in 8 years

  • Sales view missing for materials with open SD history

  • Base UoM inconsistent with alternative UoM

Why This Is Mandatory

S/4HANA:

  • Performs real-time MRP

  • Uses simplified inventory tables (MATDOC)

  • Enforces stricter checks on material consistency

Rationale

Migrating unused or inconsistent materials:

  • Increases migration volume

  • Slows performance

  • Causes MRP and ATP errors

Selective transitions benefit most by reducing material footprint.

3. Chart of Accounts & G/L Accounts

What Needs Cleaning

  • Blocked or unused G/L accounts

  • Accounts without proper account type

  • Inconsistent descriptions across company codes

  • Legacy reconciliation accounts

ECC Example

  • G/L 799999 created for testing, never used

  • Same G/L has different names in different company codes

Why This Is Mandatory

S/4HANA:

  • Introduces Universal Journal (ACDOCA)

  • FI and CO are merged

  • Reconciliation accounts behave differently

Rationale

Dirty G/L data leads to:

  • Conversion errors

  • Financial reconciliation issues

  • Incorrect financial statements

In selective transitions, only active and relevant accounts should be migrated.

4. Open Financial Transactions (AR/AP)

What Needs Cleaning

  • Cleared documents not properly closed

  • Open items with incorrect statuses

  • Residual items with zero balance

  • Old parked documents

ECC Example

  • Invoice marked as open but cleared manually

  • Zero-balance open items still flagged as open

Why This Is Mandatory

S/4HANA:

  • Performs real-time reconciliation

  • No longer tolerates technical inconsistencies

  • Universal Journal requires exact balances

Rationale

Open AR/AP items are always migrated in selective scenarios. Incorrect open items cause:

  • Balance mismatches

  • Failed migrations

  • Post-go-live reconciliation chaos

5. Asset Accounting (FI-AA)

What Needs Cleaning

  • Assets without acquisition values

  • Assets without depreciation areas

  • Retired assets still marked as active

  • Incomplete asset master records

ECC Example

  • Asset created but never capitalized

  • Depreciation area missing for a company code

Why This Is Mandatory

S/4HANA:

  • Uses New Asset Accounting only

  • Fully integrated into Universal Journal

  • Legacy asset inconsistencies cannot be converted

Rationale

Selective transitions often migrate only active assets. Cleaning ensures:

  • Correct depreciation

  • Accurate asset balances

  • Compliance with S/4 FI-AA model

6. Open Logistics Transactions

What Needs Cleaning

  • Open purchase orders with no activity

  • Incomplete deliveries

  • Open production orders never closed

  • Inconsistent goods movements

ECC Example

  • PO created 6 years ago, no GR, no IR

  • Production order technically complete but not closed

Why This Is Mandatory

S/4HANA:

  • Uses MATDOC for inventory

  • Enforces strict document flow

  • Real-time stock valuation

Rationale

Open logistics documents are technically migrated, not recreated. Dirty data causes:

  • Stock inconsistencies

  • Migration failures

  • Incorrect inventory valuation

7. Customizing and Organizational Data

What Needs Cleaning

  • Unused plants, storage locations

  • Inactive company codes

  • Invalid assignments (plant to company code)

  • Legacy controlling areas

ECC Example

  • Plant exists but no postings in 10 years

  • Company code included but not in scope

Why This Is Mandatory

Selective transitions rely on precise scoping. Invalid org data:

  • Complicates data slicing

  • Causes migration logic conflicts

  • Increases testing effort

Rationale

Clean org structures ensure:

  • Correct selective extraction

  • Lean S/4 system

  • Lower total cost of ownership

8. Custom Code & Z-Objects (Indirect Data Impact)

What Needs Cleaning

  • Z-tables with obsolete data

  • Custom programs using removed tables

  • Hard-coded company codes or years

ECC Example

  • Z-table referencing BKPF/BSEG directly

  • Custom report using VBFA incorrectly

Why This Is Mandatory

While not migrated as data:

  • Custom code processes migrated data

  • Dirty Z-data can break business processes

Rationale

Selective data migration often includes selective Z-data. Cleaning avoids:

  • Runtime errors

  • Incorrect reporting

  • Post-go-live defects

Understand your Business "WHY" for DATA CLEARLY

A Selective S/4HANA transition magnifies data quality issues because only parts of the system are migrated. S/4HANA’s simplified architecture does not tolerate inconsistencies that ECC allowed for decades.

Mandatory Data Cleaning Ensures:

  • Successful migration runs

  • Accurate financial reconciliation

  • Stable business processes

  • Long-term S/4HANA performance

Key Takeaway:

Data cleansing is not a technical task—it is a business transformation enabler.

#S4HANA #DATA #DATACLEANSING #MandatoryS4HANADATAOBJECTS #SAPECCtoS4HANA #SelectiveTransformation #DataQuality #ERPTransformation

#LegacyModernization

entry.title: Learn with the experts - The new SAP Learning Community for Data and Analytics
entry.content:
Last month SAP launched the new learning community for Data and Analytics. This community is aimed at anyone who is working through SAP learning content or perhaps preparing for certification. You can ask questions, read insightful blog posts, and stay up to date with learning content announcements such as new or updated courses, certifications, links to deep-dive webinars and other learning events. 

This community provides you with access to the entire team of SAP Data and Analytics instructors who develop the courses and certifications, and of course, deliver the SAP classes. They regularly write blog posts in the community and will answer your questions to ensure you make good progress with your learning. 

The solutions that we cover in this community include:

  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP BW and SAP BW/4HANA
  • SAP BusinessObjects
  • SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics
  • SAP HANA Modeling and Data Provisioning
  • Developing data models using ABAP CDS

If you run into trouble whilst working through learning content, just post a question and one of the team will get back to you.  

Please note that the Data and Analytics community provides support relating specifically to the learning content of data and analytics solutions. SAP are currently launching learning communities covering all other SAP solutions. Click here to get to the home page where you see all available learning communities. If you don't see the community you are looking for, come back later. More are being added each week. 

If your question is related to learning but not specifically about data and analytics topics, we also have a learning community for general questions. For example, questions relating to your Learning Hub subscription or certifications in general.

So connect with the SAP learning content development and instructor team today by joining the new SAP Data and Analytics learning community.

IMPORTANT: When you reach the community, don't forget to hit the JOIN button so that you don't miss out on content.

entry.title: Introducing the SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit
entry.content:
Focus of this blog:

  • Introduction
  • How the UI Kit is set up
  • How to get the UI Kit
  • How to use the UI Kit
  • Essential Ressources

 

Introduction

The new SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit is a high-fidelity Figma kit specifically designed for creating dashboards in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories. It closely reflects Stories UI elements and is based on the SAP Fiori Morning Horizon theme.

The UI Kit includes ready-to-use dashboard templates with placeholder or example data, charts, and other UI controls. Designers can easily switch between templates, swap visualization types, and adapt dashboards as requirements evolve. This accelerates the design process while supporting a seamless transition from mock-ups to implementation and ensuring consistent results across projects.

👉 Download the UI Kit from GitHub

 

 

 

Why Use the SAC Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit?

  • Efficiency: Reduce repetitive design efforts with pre-built components, saving time and resources.
  • High-Fidelity Mockups: Design detailed and realistic dashboards that closely reflect the SAP Analytics Cloud capabilities.
  • Consistency: Adhere to established design standards to produce a cohesive look across various dashboards, enhancing overall user experience.

 

 

How the UI Kit is set up

The UI Kit consists of three pages for easy navigation and use.

The first page, “📓 Cover“, is a UI Kit overview that includes a cover, and a slide explaining the purpose and usage of the UI Kit, as well as semantic versioning.

"Cover" Page of the SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit

The second page, “✏️ Analytical Pages”, provides dashboard templates. Depending on the purpose of your dashboard, you can choose to use different BI page types, or start with an empty page to design your dashboard from scratch.

"Analytical Pages" page of the SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit

The third page, “✏️ Components”, contains all other assets: UI elements and controls, containers and different chart types available in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

"Components" page of the SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit

 

How to get the UI Kit

  1. Navigate to the "SAC Fiori Horizon Theme" folder on GitHub.

  2. Open the file "SAC_Hi-Fi_Dashboard_UI_Kit.fig".
    Open the "SAC Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit" Figma File from GitHub
  3. Click on the Download button.
    Download the "SAC Dashboard Hi-Fi UI Kit" Figma File from GitHub
